abstract:
The electroreduction of CO on oxide-derived copper (OD-Cu) is driven by metastable surface features that exhibit unusually strong CO binding. In this study, OD-Cu electrodes synthesized via the $H_2$ reduction of $Cu_2O$ precursors demonstrated high catalytic performance, converting CO to acetate and ethanol with nearly 50% Faradaic efficiency at moderate overpotentials. Using temperature-programmed desorption (TPD), we identified unique surface sites on OD-Cu that bind CO more tightly than the standard terrace and stepped sites found on polycrystalline Cu foil. 

The catalytic activity of these materials is highly sensitive to microstructure; thermal annealing at 350 °C caused a 44-fold drop in the surface-area-corrected current density and reduced the Faradaic efficiency for CO reduction to less than 5%. This deactivation directly correlates with a significant decrease in the density of the strong CO-binding sites. By integrating these findings with electron nanodiffraction mapping, we propose that the high activity and selectivity of OD-Cu stems from metastable sites supported by grain boundaries. Identifying these specific surface features represents a critical step toward tailoring the surface chemistry required for efficient electrochemical CO reduction.
